commentary:
**أَلْهَىٰكُمُ ٱلتَّكَاثُرُ**

The Surah begins with two powerful words that capture the essence of humanity's eternal struggle: الْهَىٰكُمُ and التَّكَاثُرُ. The translation "the want of more and more has distracted you" only scratches the surface of their profound implications.

The word لَهَا describes a specific kind of distraction where you become preoccupied with something less important at the expense of something far more significant. Imagine waiting at an airport with your flight leaving in 45 minutes. You start checking text messages or browsing social media, completely losing track of time. Before you realize it, the gate has closed and your flight has departed. That moment of distraction cost you something valuable. This is the essence of laha – when your mind wanders from what truly matters and you lose an irreplaceable opportunity.

In classical Arabic, لَهْو refers to entertainment and pastimes. It describes those activities that, while not necessarily harmful in themselves, consume our attention at the cost of our greater purpose. The linguistic root connects to the image of someone tossing away a piece of food caught between their teeth – something that has no value to them. Similarly, laha means to toss aside or put on the back burner something that actually holds great worth.

The second word, التَّكَاثُرُ, is even richer in meaning. Built on the root كَثْرَة, meaning "plenty" or "abundance," the tafa'ul form reveals four distinct implications that paint a complete picture of human behavior.

First, التَّكَاثُرُ means competing to want more. This competition arises not from genuine need but from comparison. You were completely content until you saw someone else having more, and suddenly you're at unrest. Why do they have what I don't have? This competitive spirit drives you to pursue what they possess, not because you need it, but because they have it.

Second, it encompasses showing off what you've acquired. It's not enough that you obtained more – you need them to see it. You want them to feel the same inferiority you felt when they had more than you. In some cultures, this manifests dramatically at weddings where families meticulously track what others served, how elaborate their decorations were, what gifts they gave, all with the singular purpose of outdoing them when their turn comes. Entire meetings are held to strategize this "battle" of one-upmanship.

This competition doesn't begin in adulthood. Even as children, we experience it. A classmate gets a new pencil case that looks like a robot, and suddenly your plain one seems inadequate. Someone has an eraser that smells like strawberries, and you're determined to get one that smells like watermelon. The petty nature of these competitions doesn't diminish their power over our minds.

When kids come to school in America without uniforms, the competition intensifies around brands and labels. "Where did you get those shoes? Walmart?" becomes a cutting remark. The need to have that brand, or better, consumes young minds. This extends beyond material goods to modern measures of worth: how many followers do you have? How many likes did your post get? The arena changes, but the competition remains.

Third, التَّكَاثُرُ describes wanting more even when no one else is around to compete with. Some people accumulate wealth obsessively without caring about appearances or comparisons. I've met individuals making millions annually who still wear the same worn shirt from a decade ago, who look homeless despite being CEOs with hundreds of employees. For them, it's not about competing or showing off – it's simply that what they have is never enough. They need to be doing more, earning more, acquiring more. This internal drive consumes them completely.

Fourth, التَّكَاثُرُ operates within shared cultural or social standards. We form circles – whether family, friends, or community – that become societies within society. These circles establish their own standards, likes, dislikes, what's acceptable and what's not. In New York, you find Bangladeshi communities concentrated in specific blocks where the khutbah is delivered in Bangla, and they function as their own world. London has similar ethnic neighborhoods. People form these bubbles and live within them.

Within these circles, everyone thinks alike. When a daughter reaches twenty, the entire extended family starts pressuring her about marriage because "that's when we got married in our family." After marriage, they hound her about having a baby. Every life decision gets measured against the collective standard. If you're not buying a house by a certain age, you're failing. "Your cousin bought one. You're still renting? You're making someone else rich. Just take out a mortgage, everyone does it." The pressure becomes relentless.

This is perhaps the most insidious form of التَّكَاثُرُ because it comes from loved ones. They genuinely believe they're helping you, pushing you to meet standards that will make you acceptable in their eyes. Walking away from these artificial standards requires tremendous courage. When you start taking your religion seriously, you'll face resistance from the very places you'd least expect it – sometimes violent, aggressive resistance. Most people simply give in, accepting "that's just how we do things."

Now consider what Allah ﷻ left unsaid. He says this want for more has distracted you – but distracted you from what? He doesn't specify. Prayer? Charity? Caring for those in need? Learning your religion? Seeking truth? Meeting your potential? Actually using the gifts Allah ﷻ gave you to help others and do good in the world?

The omission is deliberate and merciful. The answer differs for each person. While we all share the general purpose of worship, the specific talents, opportunities, and responsibilities Allah ﷻ gave me are not the same as those He gave you. Perhaps He blessed you with wealth, or an analytical mind, or management skills, or artistic ability. Your talents are not my talents. What Allah ﷻ gave you, you should have put to productive use.

The greatest of all these blessings is time. Some of us have the gift of speech, others of writing, others of making money or wise investments. But time is the one gift we all share equally. And what does entertainment do? It burns time. Hours fly by between episodes of a series. Video games consume entire nights. The sun rises and sets, empires could rise and fall, and you're still absorbed in whatever distraction has captured you.

Consider that our worship itself teaches time management. The five pillars of Islam include one conceptual element – the Shahadah – and four practical acts: prayer, fasting, Hajj, and zakah. Look closely at these practical acts: they're all specifically scheduled activities teaching us to manage time. Prayer occurs at particular times daily, fasting at particular times, Hajj at a particular time in your life, zakah at a particular time each year.

Allah ﷻ placed two grand objects in the sky for us: the sun and the moon. Our prayers function according to where the sun is – it's our clock. Our other worship like Hajj and fasting follow the moon – it's our calendar. The sun is our clock, the moon is our calendar. To this day, humanity manages time using these same two elements: a clock and a calendar. The entire religion is designed around time management.

The Quran highlights money and children as two primary sources of distraction: "Don't allow your wealth and your children to distract you from remembering Allah ﷻ" (63:9). A teenager might say, "I don't care about money or kids, I just want to have fun." But when you reach mature age, what occupies your mind? How much is in the bank. When you have children, your thoughts race constantly: How will I provide for them? Where will they go to school? How will I pay tuition? Can we afford this vacation? We need a bigger house. Then there's the morning commute to school, pickup, basketball practice, soccer, programming class – activity after activity consumes your days and nights.

You might think, "I'm not materialistic, I'm just raising my children well." But Allah ﷻ is saying that too can become a consuming distraction. I've met Muslim parents so obsessed with their child's Quran memorization that it becomes a trophy to display. "Can my son recite before your lecture? He's really good." No. "Just a quick recitation?" No, I want to listen to him now, but not as a performance for others to earn their praise. That six-year-old picking his nose doesn't care about being your trophy, and I won't participate in that.

This doesn't mean we should never be entertained or relax. The Prophet ﷺ gave us a balanced principle: "What is little and is enough is better than what is too much and ends up distracting you." Things should be done in measure, in proper proportion. Don't do too much of anything. Having Angry Birds on your phone won't send you to Jahannam. But if you obsess over getting perfect scores on every level, if every free second finds your fingers itching to play, if you're playing during Jumu'ah and only put it away when the Imam says "Aqimis-Salah" – that's too much.

Perhaps it's your Facebook notifications, your Instagram, your constant checking even when nothing's there. "Nothing yet. Still nothing. Let me check again. Still nothing." This constant distraction becomes a serious problem. Technology has made لَهْو (distraction) a far bigger challenge in our age than ever before in history. Our parents' generation – their distraction was hiding a storybook from teachers, waiting months for the next issue to come out. Reading was considered bad behavior. Those days are gone. We must recognize these modern distractions not as small problems, but as potentially devastating ones.

**حَتَّىٰ زُرْتُمُ ٱلْمَقَابِرَ**

This distraction will persist until you visit the graves. The Prophet ﷺ said in an authentic hadith: "If the son of Adam had two valleys full of wealth, he would be looking for a third one. Nothing will fill the belly of the son of Adam except dirt" – meaning you'll keep wanting more until dirt is poured on you in your grave. "And Allah accepts the repentance of the one who repents." Another narration states: "His mouth will never be filled except by dirt." Yet another: "The soul of the son of Adam will not be satiated until the dirt fills it."

You're going to stay this way until you die. This sounds ominous, even harsh. But sometimes loved ones speak in harsh, almost sarcastic tones to shake you from your delusion. A parent whose child has diabetes might say, "Please, keep eating sugar until it kills you!" Are they wishing death? No – they're speaking out of frustrated love, hoping the stark warning jolts their child to change. This is the tone Allah ﷻ takes: You've been so distracted, until when? Until you're heading to the graves? Is that when you'll finally stop?

The phrase زُرْتُمُ الْمَقَابِرَ – "you visited the graves" – is remarkable in its choice of words. There's a narration, though not completely authentic, that provides interesting context. Two tribes, Banu Sa'd and another, competed over everything: "We have better warriors, better history, better poetry, better food." "No, we're the best!" Eventually they argued, "Our ancestors were more noble." "We had more warriors." "Let's go to the graveyard and count the graves to prove who had more." So they went to count graves, competing even in death.

Allah ﷻ is saying: You're so obsessed with competing that this obsession took you even to a graveyard. The graveyard should be where you realize all of this is futile and pointless. The reality of death stares you in the face, yet you can't see it for what it is. Instead, you're boasting about who's better, pointing at graves. How dense can you become?

Consider what this reveals: when someone truly becomes distracted by التَّكَاثُرُ, you can give them the biggest reality check and they simply cannot see it. They transform even that into another competition. Think about how we've reduced the Quran itself. What greater reality check exists than the Quran? Yet for some, our entire relationship with it has been reduced to beautified recitation alone. Someone might say, "I've been learning Quran for ten years," meaning they've been learning tajweed for ten years. But tajweed is not Quran – recitation is a means to contemplate the Quran, not an end in itself.

When you cut a branch from its root, it has no life left. The purpose of beautiful, measured recitation was that you take your time, so your heart can be affected by each word. The more melodiously you recite, the more it affects your emotions, because melody affects emotions. It was supposed to impact your heart. When it becomes mere musical technique, when you're just counting "this madd was this long, this qalqalah was correct or incorrect," what have you turned it into? Even graves can become a distraction – that's how far gone you are.

But many scholars say this ayah doesn't necessarily refer to that specific incident. Rather, until you visit the graves means until you die, until you're being taken to the graveyard and buried. But why use the word زِيَارَة – "visit" – for death? Why not simply say "until you die" or "until you're buried in graveyards"?

We must explore the implications of زَارَ. The original meaning is to meet someone and embrace them. زَوْر in Arabic literally means chest – when chests collide in an embrace, that's زِيَارَة. When you visit someone, it means you're alive and they're alive. Some scholars use this to suggest that our time in the graves is actually a time of life, not death – a life of its own, implied by the word زِيَارَة.

Others suggest it's sarcasm. When you visit, you go on your own – you get in the car yourself, walk there yourself, knock on the door yourself. But when you're buried, are you going to the graveyard yourself? No, you're being carried, being taken. The sarcastic language says: "You'll visit graves" – but it's not really a visit you choose. It's interesting that elsewhere in the Quran, when Allah ﷻ talks about death, you're never the subject. Death acts upon you. "Death has come to you. Death arrived. The final conviction reached you." Death is always the doer, not you.

But there are other profound meanings to explore. زِيَارَة means to visit someone, to meet with them, to embrace them. It's as though Allah ﷻ is saying: Throughout history, so many have been distracted just like you are. They walked this path before you. You're headed right there to embrace them, to join the company of those poor souls who made the same mistake. You're following in their footsteps, joining the long human tradition of loss.

Another powerful dimension: when you visit someone, is it permanent or temporary? It's temporary. زِيَارَة, for a normal person, isn't long. You go to someone's house for an hour, maybe two, perhaps three. By four hours, you need to leave. (Unless you're from certain cultures where "It's getting late, I should get going" is followed by forty-five more minutes of conversation, then "Yeah, it's pretty late, I'm sure you're tired" followed by two more hours, then "I think it's pretty late, I'm gonna get going" but you still don't leave. That's not زِيَارَة, that's moving in.)

By definition, زِيَارَة means visiting someplace for a short time. Consider: this life we live on earth – fifty years, a hundred years, let's stretch it to 120 years. How long have some people been in their graves? Thousands of years. Yet that entire time, Allah ﷻ calls زِيَارَة – a brief visit. Those thousands of years Allah ﷻ describes as temporary, very brief, because something much longer is coming: the Akhirah.

If the time in the grave, which for some spans millennia, is just a زِيَارَة, what does that make the time when I'm still breathing above the earth? If the time in the grave is just a visit, then the time alive is even less – moments, breaths, nothing more. Your life is nothing but a few breaths.

Look what the words زُرْتُمُ الْمَقَابِرَ accomplish: they create urgency about how little time we have on earth. If the time in our graves is so short, what does that say about the time we have while alive? When you realize how short your time is, there's no room for distractions. That's human nature. If you're late for your flight with just enough time to reach the airport before the gates close, and someone says, "Do you want dessert? Want to call your cousin? Come here for a second," would you say "Yeah, sure"? No – "I have to catch the flight. I'll call from the plane. If the dessert's already packed, I'll take it. If not, don't pack it, I don't have time."

Nothing will distract you when something immediate and urgent looms. Time is running out, you have too much to do, no time for distractions. The sense of urgency created by حَتَّىٰ زُرْتُمُ الْمَقَابِرَ is profound.

translation: No! You are going to know
commentary:
**كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ**

كَلَّا – "No!" A forceful rejection. You are so wrong. How can you be like this? سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ – "Very soon you will know." Some say this first "you will know" refers to death itself – very soon you'll die, and then you'll know how little time you had. The Prophet ﷺ said, "Whoever dies, his resurrection has begun." Your account is closed at death.

This is Allah's stern warning, as if to say: This is your state – you've been distracted, obsessed with wanting more, and this won't stop until you end up in that grave. Let me tell you, that time in the grave is pretty short. Something far worse is coming after.

translation: Then no! You are going to know
commentary:
**ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ**

ثُمَّ – "Then..." He says it again. كَلَّا – "No!" As if to say: Perhaps you didn't hear Me the first time. In My eyes, this is inevitable. سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ – "Very soon you will know."

The first "you will know" might refer to the moment of death – that realization that life is over, nothing can be done about your fate anymore, whatever time you had to prepare for the next life is gone. You're left just thinking about that. Even if your time in the grave spans thousands of years, it feels like moments passing by.

Then another time: you're raised again. Judgment Day begins. Another realization dawns: "Oh no, this is what it was." The second knowing.

There's a difference between knowing something and truly knowing something. A student is told, "Study, or you won't pass. This is serious." "I know, I know, I know." Then they don't study. Then they fail. When they fail, they know – but it's a different level of knowing. They knew before, but there's knowing and there's knowing. سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ, ثُمَّ سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ – "You will know, then you will really know." Levels of knowing – you'll find out soon enough. Oh, you're really going to find out.

translation: No! If you only knew with knowledge of certainty
commentary:
**كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ ٱلْيَقِينِ**

كَلَّا – "No, no, no!" لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ – "Had you only known, if you were only to come to know..." Come to know what? عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ – "knowledge of certainty."

The wording is profound. "If you were to come to know knowledge" – but the word عِلْمَ is already in تَعْلَمُونَ (you know). Why mention it again? Some argue it's emphasis, but it's actually revealing something deeper. "If you actually knew what it means to truly, truly know" – knowledge that leads to unshakeable conviction.

In Arabic, يَقِين means "certain knowledge that has no doubts left, where you've verified the matter completely." عِلْمُ الْيَقِينِ is knowledge that produces absolute certainty. "If you truly knew with conviction, had you truly realized that what I'm telling you is real, is certain, is happening, with no room for doubt – that life is short, you must change, you cannot be distracted – if you truly realized that..."

لَوْ is used for something hypothetical, suggesting: you're just not going to learn, are you? It won't happen. You're a lost cause. Several elements in this Surah describe humanity as essentially a lost cause: أَلْهَىٰكُمُ in past tense, لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ with لَوْ suggesting it won't happen.

Since we're at this stage, let me explain what عِلْمُ الْيَقِينِ means. The Surah mentions التَّعْلَمُونَ twice in ayahs 3 and 4, then later we'll see عَيْنَ الْيَقِينِ. There are three phrases describing levels of knowledge and conviction. This progression between them is remarkable.

Let me explain through example rather than abstraction. You see smoke but don't see fire. Do you already know there's a fire? Yes, because if there wasn't fire, there would be no smoke. You only saw smoke, but using it as evidence, you realize that even though you haven't seen the fire, there definitely is one. You're completely satisfied. You can tell someone, "Look, there's a fire there." They might say, "No, there's no fire, that's just smoke." You'll say, "No, no, no – you can't have smoke unless there is fire. There's absolutely a fire over there."

You can infer from evidence and arrive at conviction even without seeing with your own eyes. Animals can't do this. A bird may see smoke far away but won't conclude there's fire until it goes close, sees it, and thinks "Okay, I'm going to turn around before I become barbecue pigeon."

The second kind of conviction: you actually saw the fire. If you saw the fire, you're obviously convinced there's a fire. Someone asks, "How do you know for sure there's a fire?" You say, "I saw it with my own eyes." That's not عِلْمُ الْيَقِينِ (knowledge-based conviction), that's عَيْنُ الْيَقِينِ (eyewitness account-based conviction).

But imagine you were asleep in a hotel when a fire started. You woke to smoke and flames, jumped out a window, got burns on your hands from the fire. Someone asks what happened. "There was a fire." "How do you know there was a fire?" What will you show them? "Dude, look at my face. What are these burn marks?" You didn't just know about a fire. You didn't just see a fire. You were touched by the fire. You experienced it. That's حَقُّ الْيَقِينِ – the reality of it, where the reality touched you.

There are three degrees of conviction. Allah ﷻ is complaining: You're not like animals. You're human beings. Animals will only be convinced by عَيْنُ الْيَقِينِ or حَقُّ الْيَقِينِ. Human beings were made superior – we can arrive at truth using عِلْمُ الْيَقِينِ.

But when you're distracted by only the material, the only things important to you are things you can see. Because you can see them, they're valuable. Because you cannot see them, they're not valuable. Remember in Surah Al-Qari'ah: things that should be heavy become light, things that should be light become heavy. You see the mountain is heavy, so it's heavy. You see the nation is powerful, so it's powerful. You don't see that honesty is valuable, so you don't put weight on it. You don't see truthfulness, mercy, forgiveness are valuable – you don't see these things produce material gain, so you don't believe in them. "What's the benefit? Show me the benefit. Let me see it."

Allah ﷻ is describing: if you were only the kind of person who could be convinced using knowledge, not requiring eyewitness account or personal experience, but using knowledge alone – if you could only be that way. Too bad you're the kind who's only convinced when they see.

translation: You will surely see the Hellfire
commentary:
**لَتَرَوُنَّ ٱلْجَحِيمَ**

Well, if that's what you want and how you're going to be, then I'll make sure you see it. لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ – "You will absolutely see the Jaheem." Now you're convinced? Why is seeing mentioned now? If only you could learn through knowledge. But you won't. Fine, have it your way. You only believe when you see, don't you? You will absolutely see Jaheem.

The word الْجَحِيمَ used for hell is significant. جَاحِم refers to the wide-open, dilated eyes of a lion or predator about to attack, when the pupils expand and the eyes become red with intensity. Those eyes – the last thing prey sees before the lion rips it to shreds – are called جَاحِم. That's one of hell's names.

The word choice suggests: you will be staring at hell, but hell will also be staring at you. You're not just looking at it – it's looking at you like a predator stalking its prey.

This carries an important lesson. When people are distracted, they're often distracted from something uncomfortable. You have a problem you don't want to deal with, so you drown yourself in video games, TV, movies, substances – anything to avoid thinking about the problem. You don't want to face it. People tell you, "Let's talk about it, deal with it, solve it." "No, I don't want to talk about it. I don't want to deal with it. I don't want to face it." You'd rather drown in distraction.

The ugliest reality any human being will ever face is hell. When you're on a train and someone's staring at you, you stare back uncomfortably, or even move. "Why are you looking at me like that?" When you see something uncomfortable, you look away. But on Judgment Day, we have to look at the most horrendous thing we've ever seen. And it's not just us looking at it – it's looking at us with predatory eyes, staring straight at us.

What's the natural inclination when something's staring at you? Look away. But there's nowhere else to look. You're going to look straight at it. Just like you're not visiting the grave by choice – you're being taken there – Allah ﷻ says with sarcasm: "Oh yes, you'll face it. There's no distraction now. There's no لَهْو anymore. Now you have to look straight at it."

translation: Then you will surely see it with the eye of certainty
commentary:
**ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّهَا عَيْنَ ٱلْيَقِينِ**

ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّهَا عَيْنَ الْيَقِينِ – "Then you will see it with the eye of certainty." Let me tell you, I will make you look at it with eyes of conviction. The first time, you can't even believe it's real. "Is that even real? What is that?" As you're dragged closer and closer, your view becomes clearer and clearer, and it dawns on you: This is for real. This isn't a figment of your imagination. This is actually happening.

Elsewhere in the Quran, you find "And when they see it from a distant place, they will hear its raging and roaring" (25:12). The roar of a lion – we saw its stare from a distance, now its roar. But another stage describes hell being brought close: "And hell is brought near for all to see" (26:91). It was distant (makān ba'īd), then it became brought near (burrizat), right in front of you.

These two stages: لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ – phase one, you're looking at it from a distance. ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّهَا عَيْنَ الْيَقِينِ – then you look at it with eyes of conviction, dragged close to it, staring right in front of you. May Allah ﷻ protect us from hellfire.

This raises a question: will everyone have to see hellfire? Yes. "There is not one of you but will pass over it" (19:71). Even people heading to Jannah will have to pass over Jahannam. Allah ﷻ wants us to know what we've escaped. We've escaped something. He wants us to see it. When He says لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ, He's talking to all humanity: you will absolutely see it. You'll get a close look at what you've escaped.

Even believers aren't spared that view. They will see it. This is why we'll be begging Allah ﷻ: "Our Lord, perfect for us our light" (66:8). "Just forgive us, let us pass through."

translation: Then you will surely be asked that Day about pleasure
commentary:
**ثُمَّ لَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ**

Because this ayah concerns one with criminal intent, one who was distracted, Allah ﷻ ends the Surah with a critical question. Is He describing someone who disbelieved, who attacked the Prophet ﷺ, who fought against Islam? No. What's the crime here? Being distracted. Having wrong priorities in life.

What will you be asked before hell, now that you see it right in front of you? ثُمَّ لَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ النَّعِيمِ – "Then you will be interrogated on that day about the blessings, the comforts, the luxuries you constantly enjoyed."

النَّعِيم comes from نُعُومَة, meaning softness. It's the same root that gives نَعَم – cattle – because cattle moves softly. النَّعِيم describes luxuries, comforts, ease that were always present in your life. The bed always there at home. Air conditioning always working. Electricity always available. Clothes always accessible. The fridge always stocked with drinks. These are النَّعِيم in our lives – things we don't even think about because they're just there.

You'll be interrogated about them. Why is this mentioned in the context of hell? Because those blessings weren't supposed to distract you. They were supposed to make you grateful and put you to work. Gratitude isn't saying "Alhamdulillah" then playing video games. It's "Alhamdulillah" then putting yourself to productive work, doing something meaningful with your life.

Let me share a hadith that brings this home powerfully. The Prophet ﷺ, one night, left his home late and encountered two of his companions, Abu Bakr and Umar (RA). He asked, "Why have you left your homes at this late hour?" They replied, "O Messenger of Allah, hunger. We couldn't take it anymore, we just took a walk because we're starving." The Prophet ﷺ said, "Me too! I swear by the One Who has my soul in His hands, the same thing brought me out of my home." They stood up with him and went to visit a man from the Ansar.

He wasn't home – his wife was. When she saw them, she said, "Please come in, my husband's not home, but please come in." The Prophet ﷺ asked, "Where is he?" "He just went to get some water." Not to get milk, not soda – water. He came back suddenly, saw the Prophet ﷺ and his companions at his house, and exclaimed, "Alhamdulillah! There is no one more honored than me today – I get to host these guests!"

He brought them some dates, some cheese. The Prophet ﷺ said, "Please eat from this." Then he said, "And some milk." So he slaughtered a young animal for them. They ate from the meat and drank until they were filled and satisfied.

When they were done, the Prophet ﷺ turned to Abu Bakr and Umar (RA) and said, "I swear by the One Who has my soul in His hands, you will be questioned about this blessing on the Day of Resurrection. Hunger brought you out of your homes, and you didn't return until this blessing came to you."

This is what the Prophet ﷺ instilled in the companions' hearts. Can you imagine? Compared to that, what luxury do we enjoy? If the Messenger ﷺ is telling them they'll be questioned about one meal – just one meal – and describing it as نَعِيم, an extended blessing, it should put you and me to shame. Where are our hearts when it comes to gratitude?

In another hadith, the Prophet ﷺ said: "The son of Adam's feet will not move on Judgment Day until he is asked about five things: his life and how he spent it; his youth and what he wore it out in; his wealth, how he earned it and what he spent it on; and what he acted upon from the knowledge he had." These are the questions you and I must answer before Allah ﷻ.

This Surah about removing distraction and becoming grateful to Allah ﷻ is a twin, a complement to Surah Al-'Asr. That Surah declared humanity is in loss. This Surah explains why – because we'll be asked about our blessings.

Consider the connections between At-Takathur and Al-Qari'ah. Al-Qari'ah began with الْهَاكُمْ (the distraction of wanting more). At-Takathur begins with أَلْهَىٰكُمُ التَّكَاثُرُ – they're willing to risk their lives to get more, and here they remain distracted until they actually die.

In Al-Qari'ah, the obsession was described as competition in quality (better food, better clothes, better house, better car – better, better, better). In At-Takathur, it's about quantity – التَّكَاثُرُ is about more. Actually, they're tied together in the shallow mind: maybe if they have more, it will be better. The only way to get better is to get more.

At the end of Al-Qari'ah, Allah ﷻ asked: "Does he not know when the graves are overturned?" (100:9). Here, the graves appear in the opening: حَتَّىٰ زُرْتُمُ الْمَقَابِرَ. There, Allah ﷻ asks repeatedly, "Does he not know?" as if saying no, he won't know the way he should until it's too late. Here: كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ, ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ – "No! You will know. Then no! You will know." Answering that question: Oh yes, you will know. You'll absolutely know. You're supposed to know before you hit the graves, but you won't. You're just too stubborn.

In Al-Qari'ah, Allah ﷻ mentions His knowledge: He knows where you stand. In At-Takathur, it's you who knows where you stand (سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ). The equation flips – both the accuser (Allah ﷻ) and the accused (humanity) know where they stand.

Both Surahs address human ingratitude. Al-'Asr: "Indeed, mankind is in loss" (103:2) – we're extremely ungrateful. At-Takathur: You don't escape that ingratitude – ثُمَّ لَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ النَّعِيمِ – you'll be interrogated about every blessing you were ungrateful for.

The passive verbs in Al-Qari'ah continue here. In that Surah: graves being ransacked, hearts being retrieved. Why were hearts retrieved? So they could be questioned. Here: لَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ عَنِ النَّعِيمِ – you will be interrogated about the blessings you enjoyed. The passives create a continuous argument: you will answer for your distractions.

Look at the structure of At-Takathur itself. It begins with us being distracted (أَلْهَىٰكُمُ التَّكَاثُرُ) and ends with us in court answering questions for being distracted (ثُمَّ لَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ النَّعِيمِ) – your beginning and end corresponding to each other.

In the middle, Allah ﷻ mentions knowledge twice (سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ appears twice). These people don't want to learn through knowledge – they only want to learn through seeing. So He mentions seeing twice (لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ, ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّهَا). He parallels knowledge with seeing. The two ways you will learn correspond to the two ways you will see.

Right in the middle is the heart of the problem: كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ – "If only you attained knowledge of certainty." This is the heart of the Surah, right in the middle. The structure itself teaches us: conviction through knowledge stands at the center, but we're surrounded on both sides by our failure – our distraction at the start, our accountability at the end, and our stubborn refusal to learn except through harsh experience.

May Allah ﷻ give us appreciation for the guidance and beauty of His Book. May He grant us the ability to live by its wisdom before we're forced to see its truths with our own eyes. May He make us people of عِلْمُ الْيَقِينِ, who arrive at certainty through knowledge, not those who require the harsh lesson of عَيْنُ الْيَقِينِ or حَقُّ الْيَقِينِ. And may He protect us from the distractions of التَّكَاثُرُ and make us among those who use His blessings to draw near to Him.
